perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that they will not, during the course of their association with one another or, thereafter, disclose the information to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, save in accordance with the provisions of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that they will endeavor not to, during the course of their association with one another or, thereafter, disclose the information to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, save in accordance with the provisions of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agree that they will not" with "agree that they will endeavor not to" weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. "Endeavor" implies an attempt, not a guarantee, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 (Act No. 4 of 2013) (POPIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA Section 19(1) mandates that responsible parties must secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in their possession. The original text creates a stronger obligation not to disclose information. The modified text, by using "endeavor not to", dilutes this obligation and does not guarantee the safeguarding of information as required by POPIA, potentially leading to a violation if personal information is disclosed due to a lack of stringent measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute A statute is a law enacted by a legislature. Statutes are also called acts, such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 or the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . Federal laws must be passed by both houses of Congress, the House of Representative and the Senate, and then usually require approval from the President before they can take effect. As explained by the Library of Congress , enacted federal statutes are published multiple times. First, each individual law is published as a “slip law.” Then, all of the slip laws for each session of Congress are published together as “session laws.” Finally, all laws that are of a “general and permanent nature” are eventually compiled into the United States Code , and also the Revised Statutes of the United States. State statutes can be found through the LII's page on Constitutions, Statutes, and Codes . Readers can also attempt to find state statutes organized by topic: Topical Index: State Statutes . [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS wex definitions government legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect information by changing 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to'. This creates an ambiguity that undermines the original intent. While the provided law snippet is about statutes in general, the Law Explanation states that POPIA Section 19(1) requires responsible parties to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information. The modified text, by using "endeavor not to", does not guarantee the safeguarding of information as required by POPIA, potentially leading to a violation. Therefore, the change introduces an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance, which constitutes a legal contradiction as it weakens a legally mandated obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall comply with a request, in terms of this clause 7, within 7 days of receipt of such a request
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall try to comply with a request, in terms of this clause 7, within a reasonable timeframe of receipt of such a request
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall comply within 7 days" with "shall try to comply within a reasonable timeframe" introduces significant ambiguity. "Try to comply" weakens the mandatory action to a mere attempt. "Reasonable timeframe" replaces a fixed deadline with a vague term, opening the door to disputes and delayed compliance. The original clause offers certainty and allows a party to plan accordingly, whereas the modified version does not.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 26(4) of the Companies Act addresses the right of shareholders to access company records and information. Although this NDA is not directly related to shareholder access, the principle of timely compliance with information requests is relevant. The modified text, by replacing 'shall comply within 7 days' with 'shall try to comply within a reasonable timeframe,' introduces uncertainty that could impede timely access to information, thereby undermining the spirit and broader intent of corporate transparency and accountability as promoted by the Companies Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear obligation to comply within 7 days. The changed text replaces this with "try to comply within a reasonable timeframe." The Perturbation Explanation correctly states that this change weakens the mandatory action. The original clause allowed a party to plan accordingly, whereas the modified version introduces uncertainty and does not allow a party to plan accordingly. This is an Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation and as such, it creates a legal contradiction. The scraped snippets are not helpful.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with the provisions of this agreement shall be referred to arbitration and determined in accordance with the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965, as amended from time to time,
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with the provisions of this agreement may be referred to arbitration and preferably determined with consideration to the principles outlined in the Arbitration Act 42 of 1965, as amended from time to time,
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be referred to arbitration" to "may be referred to arbitration" makes arbitration optional rather than mandatory. Adding "preferably determined with consideration to the principles outlined in the Arbitration Act" weakens the requirement to strictly adhere to the Act, allowing for deviation from established procedures. This makes the dispute resolution process uncertain and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Arbitration Act emphasizes that a written agreement to submit present or future disputes to arbitration is binding. By changing 'shall' to 'may' and suggesting 'consideration to the principles' instead of strict adherence, the modified text undermines the enforceability and binding nature of arbitration agreements, as intended by the Act. It creates uncertainty about whether the dispute will actually be resolved through arbitration and whether the Act's provisions will be strictly followed.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 3 / CHAPTER 1 / § 3 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 3 USC 3 : Number of electors Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 3-THE PRESIDENT CHAPTER 1-PRESIDENTIAL ELECTIONS AND VACANCIES Jump To: Source Credit §3. Number of electors The number of electors shall be equal to the number of Senators and Representatives to which the several States are by law entitled at the time when the President and Vice President to be chosen come into office; except, that where no apportionment of Representatives has been made after any enumeration, at the time of choosing electors, the number of electors shall be according to the then existing apportionment of Senators and Representatives. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 644, 62 Stat. 672 .)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates arbitration ('shall be referred to arbitration'), creating a binding obligation. The changed text makes arbitration optional ('may be referred to arbitration'), which undermines the binding nature of the agreement. This contradicts the intent of arbitration agreements as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1 regarding the number of electors, is irrelevant. The absence of a second snippet further limits the ability to directly tie to specific arbitration laws, but the change from mandatory to optional is itself a substantial alteration of the legal obligation.
